sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 104, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 103 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 4.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player moved RIGHT from cols 24-28 to cols 49-53 (upper-right). The pattern didn't change. The player jumped over the passage area (cols 35-37).
So the player moves to the edge of the corridor in the direction of movement. Going RIGHT from cols 24-28 moves the player to cols 49-53 (right edge).
This means the player can't stop at the passage area (cols 35-37) by going RIGHT. The player needs to approach the passage from a different direction.
Let me think about this. The passage is at rows 16-24, cols 35-37. The player is at rows 25-26, cols 49-53 (upper-right). The player can go UP from the upper-right, but the passage is to the left of the upper-right.
Actually, looking at the current image, the player is at the upper-right of the gray room. The passage is at the top-center of the gray room, connected to the black box above. The player can't reach the passage directly because the passage is above the gray room, and the player is at the upper-right of the gray room.
Wait, but the passage is at rows 16-24, which is above the gray room (rows 25-49). The player is at rows 25-26, which is the top of the gray room. So the player can go UP from rows 25-26 to reach the passage at rows 16-24.
But the player is at cols 49-53, and the passage is at cols 35-37. So the player needs to go LEFT to cols 35-37 and then UP.
Let me try going LEFT from the upper-right to reach the passage.
Actually, I realize I need to reconsider the game mechanics. The player moves to the edge of the corridor in the direction of movement. So going LEFT from cols 49-53 moves the player to cols 14-18 (left edge). But the passage is at cols 35-37, which is between the left and right edges.
So the player can't stop at the passage by going LEFT or RIGHT. The player needs to approach the passage from below (going UP).
But the player is at rows 25-26, which is the top of the gray room. The passage is at rows 16-24, which is above the gray room. So the player can go UP from rows 25-26 to reach the passage at rows 16-24.
But the player is at cols 49-53, and the passage is at cols 35-37. So the player needs to be at cols 35-37 to go UP into the passage.
Wait, but the player can't stop at cols 35-37 by going LEFT or RIGHT. The player moves to the edge of the corridor. So how can the player reach the passage?
Let me think about this differently. The gray room has a U-shaped passage. The player moves along the passage. The passage has:
- Upper part: rows 25-29, cols 14-53
- Lower part: rows 30-49, cols 14-53
- Wall: rows 30-44, cols 24-33
The wall divides the lower part into left and right sections. The upper part is open.
The player can move along the upper part (rows 25-29) from left to right or right to left. The player can also move down into the lower part (rows 30-49) from the upper part.
The passage to the black box is at rows 16-24, cols 35-37, which is above the gray room. The player can reach the passage by going UP from the upper part of the gray room (rows 25-29) at cols 35-37.
But the player can't stop at cols 35-37 by going LEFT or RIGHT. The player moves to the edge of the corridor. So how can the player reach the passage?
Actually, I think the player CAN go UP from any position in the upper part of the gray room. The player doesn't need to be at cols 35-37 to go UP. The player can go UP from cols 49-53, and the player will move UP along the right edge of the gray room.
But the passage is at cols 35-37, which is to the left of the right edge. So going UP from cols 49-53 won't reach the passage.
Let me try a different approach. Let me go UP from the current position (cols 49-53) to see where the player ends up.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 25-26, cols 49-53 (upper-right)
# Let me try going UP to see where the player ends up

result = action(['UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP', 'UP'])
print(f"Result: done={result.get('done')}, level_completed={result.get('level_completed')}")

# Check player position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'O' and n['pixels'] > 5:
        b = n['boundary']
        min_row = min(p[0] for p in b)
        max_row = max(p[0] for p in b)
        min_col = min(p[1] for p in b)
        max_col = max(p[1] for p in b)
        print(f"Player at rows {min_row}-{max_row}, cols {min_col}-{max_col}")

# Check bottom-left pattern
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nBottom-left pattern:")
for i in range(55, 61):
    print(f"  {ascii_lines[i][2:10]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Result: done=False, level_completed=False
Player at rows 25-26, cols 49-53

Bottom-left pattern:
  BbbbbbbB
  BbbbbbbB
  BBBBBbbB
  BBBBBbbB
  BbbBBbbB
  BbbBBbbB
